sentences.text: After that, we were lined up.
And they were taking us to a building, a large building.
And at that time, we were still with the people that we came with from my hometown-- most of them, not only our hometown, but around our city.
We went into this building and it was chaos.
It was total chaos.
Everybody was crying and screaming.
They didn't know what was happening.
But I did remember one thing.
I said to my mother, if somebody is gonna ask us to help-- help them to do something, I said I'm going to volunteer.
She said, don't you dare to do it.
I mean that was my mother is don't you dare to do it.
Sure enough, in a few minutes while we were there about maybe a half hour, a German SS woman came in.
And she asked, who would like to help to bring in some food from the outside?
They needed 25 people to help her.
And my hand went up.
And my mother was crying.
She didn't want to go.
And I just pulled her with me.
And I pulled my sister with me.
I says, yes.
We are going to help them to bring some food in.
Because probably they're hungry, and so are we.
I said, let's go.
And so anyway, they took us, 25 of us, took us away.
And instead of going and bringing in some food, they took us to a different place, a totally different place, a different lager.
It's called a lager.
They took us away from all our friends to someplace where we didn't know a soul.
And that was the first thing that we noticed that don't [BACKROUND NOISE] trust them.
Of course, we didn't trust them anyway.
They took us into another lager.
It was A lager.
When we walked into this place, it was already full.
There was three layers of bunk beds in this place.
And there was only one small place available, which was on a very bottom.
And there was no flooring.
It was just the earth.
And they says, this is where you're going to be sitting, and this is where you're going to be living.
This is where you're going to be sleeping and living.
So that was our first day in Auschwitz.
Later on that day, they didn't pay any attention to us.
We were just sitting.
We weren't talking.
We were afraid to say anything at all.
Because we didn't know what to expect.
There was one woman that's called a kapo, who is in charge of the-- that camp that building.
And I happened to talk to her.
And I said to her, gee, I says, you know when we came through, I saw some buildings that had large smokestacks.
And I said, is this a factory?
Are we going to be working in a factory, or is this maybe a bakery or something?
She says, yes, it is a bakery.
And I want to tell you what kind.
I said, oh really?
And I was very naive.
And I said, what kind of bakery is it?
She says, who did you come with here?
I says, I came with my father and my aunt and my grandmother.
And she says, well, how old was your father?
And I told her how old.
He was about 50.
She says, and how about your grandmother?
I said my grandmother was 83.
She says, well, do you see that big smokestack?
She says, that's where they are going out from.
And I said, what do you mean?
She says, well, you know they-- they were killed.
And they were gassed and they were killed, and they-- they burned them.
And that's how I found out what was going on in Auschwitz.
That was my first day of introduction to Auschwitz.
And from then on it was like a living hell.
Because early in the morning, we didn't sleep that night.
Because nobody could sleep on a floor.
And I wasn't-- excuse me-- I wasn't used to sleeping on earth-- on a cold floor.
And it was night.
It wasn't summertime yet.
It was still end of April.
And we didn't sleep that night.
The next morning we had to have-- they call it Zahlappell.
Zahlappell means you have to-- we went outside and you lined up five deep.
So what I did, I put my sister in the front, and my mother in the middle, and I was behind.
And we would take turns each day.
I was in the front, my mother was always in the middle.
And my sister was in the back, and to count us.
And the first day everybody was standing outside very, very early in the morning.
And an SS man came out.
There were quite a few, but there was one particular guy, a tall-- not very tall but he was-- had a very nice posture and a good-looking guy.
And he would look us over, and look and look.
And he would select people that he didn't like.
And he pulled out quite a few people.
I had no idea for what, until I found out later what it was for.
And, but each morning, that's what happened.
And after those people were gone, they said we are-- you are going to be eating breakfast, food.
Well, food consisted of black coffee and nothing else.
And the black coffee was given to us in-- like a saucepan, a pot for five people.
So the first one start to drink, and the second one, down to the fifth.
If the fifth didn't have enough, I mean, it was just too bad.
But that-- that was our very first breakfast in-- in Auschwitz.
And we didn't do too much that day.
They-- they left us outside [BACKROUND NOISE] most of the day.
At lunchtime they came.
And I saw a lot of people in a very bad shape around me.
And for lunchtime, again they gave us a pot with some soup.
And I saw some funny things floating on the top.
I had no idea what it was.
But you were so hungry that you didn't care what was in that soup.
If you were able to just drink a little, because there was nothing to eat in that soup at all, you did it.
I mean you-- we ate.
We drank some of it.
And my mother did.
I made sure that my mother had some.
And that was our lunch.
That was the first day.
For dinner, again, they left us outside most of the time.
And the first day for dinner, they again-- they told us to line up.
We lined up.
And they told us we were going to be fed again.
And at that time they gave us a piece of-- piece of bread, a very small piece of bread.
It looked like pumpernickel to me.
It was like almost like an multi-grain piece a very thin piece of bread, and a very thin salami, one slice of salami that was like paper thin.
And that was our dinner.
And from after that, they locked us into our-- back into the barrack.
We had to go back in a barrack.
They locked us in there until next morning.
And this went on for a while.
And one day-- and every morning when we-- when we lined up for zahlappell, I knew that somebody is going to be missing the next day, and which was-- that's exactly what happened.
And I try to-- every time my-- my-- Mengele came around, and if my mother was pale, I would go in the back and I would pinch her a little bit.
And it brought a little color into her face.
Because it hurt her.
So I hurt her.
And it brought a little color into her face, just to make sure that they're not gonna take her away from me.
Because she was very, very pale.
And I did it every single day, day after day, to make sure that she was still with us.
But I saw a lot of people being taken out of the lines.
And I never saw them again, until I found out later on what was happening to them.
And one day, it-- we were there maybe two weeks.
And one day they came and they selected a few people.
We didn't know for what, and I thought to myself, they're going to select us for-- for work.
I'm going to volunteer again.
And naturally, my mother-- it didn't go well over with my mother.
She didn't like it.
But I did anyway.
And they came, and they actually-- it wasn't even that much up to me.
It was up to them to select us.
So they selected, thank god, the three of us.
And they took us to a large barn, and we didn't know what to expect.
And there were people sitting at a table with-- like a small, a small table, a chair and a small table.
And they had a needle in their hands, and we had no idea what was-- what was the needle for.
Until they said, now you-- we're going to give you a number, so that we can keep-- you know, we know where you are, and who are.
And we had to give them our name.
And then they-- they gave us the tattoo.
They tattoos us, all three of us.
And I remember my mother crying and my sister was crying very, very bitterly.
And I said to them, you know something?
I says, you know, don't cry that hard.
I said, don't cry.
Because the reason that they gave you a number is probably because they're gonna keep track of you.
Maybe, you know, if we have a number, maybe we're gonna be working, and that-- maybe that is a good sign.
So, that's kind of, you know, my mother realized that maybe I was right.
And that was our first day of having a tattoo on our arms.
And we got tattoos, consecutive numbers.
What numbers were they?
The numbers were 6731, 6732, and 6733, which I have the 6733.
Those were the numbers.
After that, the following day they collected 250 women from our barrack, who had the numbers.
And they took us away to a different place, which-- ooh-- happened to be called the Brzezinka.
We didn't know what it was.
We had no idea where it was-- where we went.
All we had to do is stuff some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] actually, something for-- to make a bed.
Excuse me.
It was like a mattress.
And they-- we had to stuff it with-- with-- straw.
So we are stuffing these mattresses with straw, and we didn't know why.
I mean they took us every single day.
So we did it.
Excuse me.
I'm sorry.
I have frog in my throat.
This went on for about three or four days.
It was back and forth, and people were so jealous of us.
All the rest of the people in our camp, because we were given white kerchiefs for our heads.
We were bald at that point.
We were all bald, naturally.
And so, we had these kerchiefs.
And we looked a little bit cleaner.
And the third day what they did, they gave us different dresses.
It was-- my-- my dress was a burgundy with white spot-- spots, actually white--
Polka dots, and that was my-- my dress.
And my mother got the same color, and so did my sister.
So they actually-- they separated us in two sections.
There were some girls, they were given polka dot-- white polka dots with a navy blue background.
Ours was with a burgundy background.
And each day, when we were going to work into-- we found out what was the name of that place.
It was Brzezinka.
And after the third or fourth day, they kept us there permanently.
And that was the start of our working within a crematorium.
We didn't know what was-- what were we supposed to do the first day.
We were still stuffing the mattresses.
And once the mattresses were all stuffed, we found out those were for us to sleep on.
Because we had two barracks.
One was the navy blue dress barrack.
One was the-- the red dress barrack.
And following day, they took us into this barrack.
And the site was only-- and the stench was unreal.
We saw clothing all the way to the ceiling.
It was like a barn, all the way to the ceiling.
And they told us, you have to take one piece at a time.
And take the hemline apart, and take the-- the buttons that were covered.
If we saw any covered buttons, we had to take the covered buttons apart.
Because what we found out, a lot of the covered buttons had gold pieces in them instead of being buttons.
This is how they were hoping that they'll be able to utilize their monies.
And I found out that all this clothing was-- came from the cattle cars, and from the crematorium.
And this is what we start to do.
We had to separate men's, women's, and children's clothing.
They had like handbags, leather handbags.
We had to cut them open, because on the bottom of a lot of the handbags they had a lot of jewelries tucked in-- inside.
So we had to take those apart, the hemlines apart.
And whatever was suspicious, wherever we thought that there could be some jewelry in, we had to take.
And we found a lot of very valuable jewelry in them.
And naturally, we had to put that in a box, and we had to turn it over to the German.
Because every day, after we were through working, they came around to get all the jewelry from us.
And this went on for a long, long time, I mean, many days.
Very, very closely, yes.
There was a guard in front of each door, every single time.
When we were in-- in Brzezinka, we saw people, transports coming in, and they were walking.
Because they were walking toward the-- toward the gas chamber.
We were seeing people going in, never coming out.
The stench was absolutely horrible.
Because you smelled the burning flesh day and night, all the time.
There was one transport that was-- it stands out in my mind that hurts me very much.
There were old people walking toward the gas chamber.
And I happened to go at that time, I happened to go for water.
They-- I was selected to go for water.
And I went for water.
And this old lady was crying, water, water.
And I was about to turn around and give her some water, when the SS man came over to me and he slapped my face so hard.
Then I thought I wasn't gonna have a tooth left in my mouth.
And I-- I did not grant her last wish.
That was very hurtful for me.
I-- I-- it was hurting me very much.
There were a lot of things happened.
I was very sick at one time.
I had a very high temperature.
And they had an infirmary-- infirmary in the building, within our building.
And they took me there.
And I know my temperature was very, very high.
And I know a lot of girls that went into that infirmary before, and I never saw them again.
They took 'em away, and I never, ever saw them again.
So what I did one day, and I was there like for three days, they gave me the thermometer.
And I looked at the thermometer, and my-- my temperature was very high.
So before the nurse was-- had a chance to come back and-- and look at me again, I took the thermometer.
I shook it down.
And I rubbed it on my-- on the sheet on my bed a little bit, just to the point when I felt it was safe.
And when she came in, I says, oh, I says, I'm sorry I took it out before you had a chance to-- you know to come in.
I said, because I had it in my mouth for an awful long time.
I said, here's the thermometer.
I said, do you want to see it?
She says, yes.
And she looked at it.
She says, oh, it looks like your temperature has gone down.
She said, but I'll have to keep you here for another day.
And I did the same thing.
I repeated the same process, just to make sure.
In the meantime, I was burning up.
Because I had a very, very high temperature.
And after the second day, they let me go out.
And I went back to work with my mother and my sister.
But while we were standing in Zahlappell, I passed out from the high temperature, I guess.
And my sister caught me.
Because I was falling back.
She caught me, and she caught me just in time, as the German passed our Zahlappell.
He counted us.
And he went right past me.
And that's when I passed out.
So that was my luck.
Otherwise, I would not be here to tell you this story.
It was very beautiful, very nice.
We were just friends.
We helped each other.
Anybody that needed some help, we did.
In fact, there was one little girl who was all by herself.
We took her with us.
And-- and we helped her as much as possible.
Because the food was a lot better than it was in the A lager.
Because, I guess, we needed a little more energy to work.
So they-- they treated us a little bit better.
And-- I forgot to tell you that each day after work, they took us to the shower.
We had to take a shower.
Because they were afraid of any-- because we got in touch with so many-- we handled so many different clothing.
And there were a lot of germs.
So they took us for us to take a shower every single day.
So that was a big plus for us, as-- as far as we were concerned.
Yes.
Yes, it is.
Yeah, the Kanada was where people would come in from other-- other lagers, and-- and take the clothing that we bundled up.
We made it into a bundle.
And they would take it out, and supposedly they-- they took all that-- all those-- that clothing into Germany to use it for whatever they were going to use it for.
I don't know.
But that's what we did for a while, yes, for a long time.
We had to make bundles.
And one-- one day I was assigned to go to a-- like a heap of eyeglasses.
I mean it was-- the eyeglasses were like in a mound, I mean, huge.
There was separate eyeglasses.
There were shoes.
That there were-- there was hair.
There was everything.
And I was-- I started-- they asked me to select certain eyeglasses, which I started to do.
And I guess I didn't do a very good job of it.
Because they came and they-- they-- they smacked me across my face so bad, because I wasn't doing a good job.
I couldn't go dig-- dig deep enough to get each-- each sunglass separately, you know, to make it look perfect.
Because those were not perfect eyeglasses.
And that's what they expected of me, to find perfect eyeglasses.
So I went back.
I was lucky that I went back to work with my mother and my sister.
Then one day, [SIGHS] again, I wasn't feeling good, and my sister decided to help me out.
Because I was weak.
And as we were working in our-- our building, we found some potatoes that were, you know, brought in from the cattle cars between the clothing.
So I went.
And as she says to me, you know something?
I'm going to cook some potatoes for you.
I said, well, I say, if you want to.
That was a very dangerous thing to do.
Because I didn't know how she was going to do it.
So we found some pots and pans in between everything that they took off the cattle cars.
And she says, I'm going to try to help you.
She took the potato, and somehow she made a makeshift little oven, or-- or-- or a stove.
And she found some candles.
And she started to cook a potato for me.
And a German SS happened to walk into our barrack at-- just at that moment.
And that was the worst thing that she could have done.
Because they took her outside.
They shaved her head again.

They beat her up very, very badly, and they made her kneel down on gravel, on very sharp, sharp gravel, from early morning on until 6:00, 7 o'clock that night, [BACKROUND NOISE] without any food, without any-- any water.
She-- they didn't give her anything.
And she was still lucky.
Because a lot of people were shot right there on the spot, and she was not.
I don't know why.
Because maybe she had-- she was blonde and blue eyes.
And they tried to spare her.
But that was-- she did that for me, and I was forever grateful to her for that.
She suffered an awful lot because of me.
She did a lot of things for me that she should not have done.
There was no place to go.
We just stayed in that small compound.
We-- we didn't go anywhere.
We went from our-- our barrack, where we-- where we lived, where we stayed, into the barrack where we worked, and to the shower, and back into our-- you know, place where we lived.
No, mm-mm nothing.
Never, no, no.
Mm-mm.
No, we always-- we had to turn everything over.
But I don't know if I told you that I took some of the very valuables and I buried them in the ground.
Because I didn't want the Germans to have everything.
Because we found an awful lot, lot of valuables, a lot of valuables.
And there was one German oberscharfuhrer that for some reason, must have taken a liking to-- to my mother and-- and the two of us.
Because they weren't supposed to know that we were related.
And he somehow had a funny feeling that we were standing always together that we might be related somehow.
So one day, he came by, and he looked at us.
And he says, oh mother with her two daughters.
And my mother said, yes.
Because she wasn't-- she couldn't lie.
She wasn't supposed to lie.
If she would have lied, if she would have said no, not mother with her two daughters, then it would have been a very bad thing.
Because I know somebody-- it happened to somebody who lied.
And they were taken away and never seen again.
So that was one of the good things that happened to us in that compound, in Brzezinka.
He walked away.
He didn't do anything.
He didn't do anything.
No, no male prisoners at all.
No, not-- not within our compound, no.
The only male prisoners we saw were the prisoners that came in.
That's called the scheisskommando, which means they were cleaning the latrines.
So they would come in with a wagon, and clean it out.
And those were the only men that, you know, we ever saw really in-- in our compound, within Brzezinka, where we were anyway.
That's how I found my cousin one day.
The scheisskommando was coming through.
And I looked at him.
I wasn't-- I kind of-- I was peaking out.
And I wasn't supposed to.
But I was peaking out.
Because the guard happened to walk away from our door, and I was peaking out.
And there was a young boy.
And I looked at him.
And it was my cousin.
And I found him there.
And I was able to help him.
He needed shoes.
I looked down.
I never talked to him, because we weren't allowed naturally.
And I saw that he needed a pair of shoes.
So we-- where we were, we were able to find a lot of shoes where we're working.
So I hid a pair of shoes for him.
And one day I-- I said to my-- my guard.
I said, you know, I says, I-- I have to go.
Do you mind?
I says, I-- I have to go.
Because we had no facilities.
And there was just one facility not too far from us.
And as my cousin was walking by, as they were coming with the scheisskommando, I had the pair of shoes under my dress, and-- and I was holding it very close to my body.
And I was walking by him.
I said to him very quickly, a pair of shoes.
And I dropped it.
And he picked it up.
And that's how he got a pair of shoes from me in Auschwitz, which was very unusual.
It was very risky for you.
Yes, very risky, but I had to do it.
Because I couldn't watch him walk around with shoes that had no soles.
His-- his-- his toes were sticking out.
So I felt sorry for him.
And I had to help him.
That was the only way for me to help him.
In Brzezinka, we were there until January 18 of 1945.
But before that, I have to tell you the things that we were lined up, a few-- you see, when you are in Brzezinka in that particular place, we saw too much.
We heard too much, and we weren't supposed to get out of there alive.
We knew it that we were not going to be getting out of there alive.
So one day we were lined up.
And I said goodbye to my mother, and I said goodbye to my sister, and to my friends that I-- I loved.
And as we were standing there and waiting to be taken away, all of a sudden an order came in again, going back into the barracks.
Why?
Because another transport was coming, and they needed our help again.
So we turned around, and we went back to work some more, and find some more clothing, and find-- I found an infant, a dead infant between the clothing in-- in our barrack.
And that was a terrible shock to me.
Because that was a horrible-- I remember my-- my relatives, and they had babies.
And I thought to myself, this could have been one of them.
But I know that they went away, too.
I mean, they-- they were killed too, with my-- with my aunts and my cousins, and my uncles who-- who looked old.
